exemplar.title: A partial replication of Loftus and Palmer (1974) to see the effect language formation makes on reconstructive memory

detailedExemplarData.title: A partial replication of Loftus and Palmer (1974) to see the effect language formation makes on reconstructive memory

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear operationalization of independent and dependent variables
Relevant reference to reconstructive memory and schema theory
weaknesses: Link between Bartlettâs schema theory and the verb manipulation is not fully articulated
Minor conceptual inaccuracies (e.g., vague use of âconfirmation biasâ and âstereotypesâ)
Relevance rationale is brief and could be more explicitly tied to teenage sampling
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction demonstrates strong hypothesis formulation and correctly operationalizes variables, with a clear aim and appropriate theoretical grounding. However, minor conceptual slips, a lack of explicit linking of schema theory to the verb effect, and a somewhat cursory justification of relevance prevent it from reaching the top twoâband (5â6) performance. A midâband score of 4 reflects solid work with room to deepen theoretical connections and relevance explanation.

strengths: Independentâmeasures design is not only described but justified to reduce demand characteristics
Sampling method and participant characteristics are clearly presented
A comprehensive list of controlled variables shows careful planning
weaknesses: Explanation of materials lacks detail on how each control extraneous variables
Limited discussion of potential sampling bias from using a single class
Ethical oversight (e.g., formal approval) is not sufficiently addressed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section provides a clear design description and robust sampling details, justifying key methodological choices. However, the rationale for material controls is underdeveloped, and ethical and generalizability considerations are only briefly mentioned. This performance sits at the top of the middle band (3/4), earning 3 marks out of 4.

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (MannâWhitney U) statistics
Graphical presentation is correctly labelled and directly addresses the hypothesis
Findings are stated, linked to hypothesis, and null hypothesis is explicitly rejected
weaknesses: No discussion of effect size or statistical assumptions to justify choice of analyses
Minor mischaracterization of Cohenâs d as âtrivialâ despite a large effect
Assumptions of MannâWhitney U (e.g., distribution shape) are not mentioned
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis applies suitable descriptive and inferential statistics and presents a clear graph that addresses the hypothesis. Interpretation correctly links findings to predictions. Yet, lack of effectâsize discussion and omission of test assumptions indicate it falls short of flawless execution. A solid 4 out of 6 reflects accurate analytic work with scope to strengthen reporting rigor.

strengths: Findings discussion ties results to reconstructive memory and schema activation theory
Multiple strengths and limitations are identified and clearly explained
One modification (use of virtual reality) is explicitly linked to ecological validity limitation
weaknesses: Discussion of confirmation bias and stereotypes lacks supporting evidence or references
Other identified limitations (sample size, seating) are not matched with justified modifications
Theoretical discussion is accurate but lacks critical nuance
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation demonstrates an ability to connect results to theory and to articulate relevant strengths and limitations. A single wellâjustified modification shows awareness of improvement. However, unsupported claims, incomplete linkage of additional modifications, and limited critical depth place it in the midâband. A score of 3 out of 6 appropriately reflects this balanced but improvable evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart depicting mean speeds for each condition is included, clearly labelled with axes and units, directly comparing the IV levels in line with the hypothesis.  Presentation is technically correct, though styling is basic; consequently a sound 5 is appropriate.

The student states the statistical outcome (U = 23.5, p < .05), explicitly rejects the null hypothesis, and links the direction of the difference to their prediction about verb intensity.  Interpretation is accurate but concise, earning 5 rather than 6.

Both IV and DV are explicitly stated and operationalised (verb used: âsmashedâ vs âcontactedâ; speed estimate in km/h).  A clear null and research hypothesis are provided.  The operational definitions are precise, satisfying all top-band requirements; therefore a 6 is awarded.

Sampling method (opportunity/convenience) is identified and the choice is explained with reference to accessibility, age, and anticipated responsibility of participants.  Characteristics (age, schooling programme, gender balance) are fully presented.  This coverage merits the top of the 3-4 band.

Reconstructive memory and schema theory are described with reference to Bartlett, Piaget and Anderson.  The student links these concepts to the predicted verb effect, explaining that words such as âsmashedâ activate a schema for higher speed.  Minor conceptual inaccuracies (e.g., spelling/grammar slips, vague mention of âconfirmation bias and stereotypesâ) prevent this from being a flawless explanation, so a lower-band 5 is the best fit.

Findings are related to reconstructive memory theory and schema activation, with some discussion of how verb choice may act as misinformation.  While this moves beyond mere description, the theoretical treatment is brief and not critically nuanced; the upper score of the 3-4 band fits best.

The student clearly states the aim â to investigate whether different verbs in a leading question influence teenagersâ speed estimates â and explicitly relates this to eyewitness testimony and the broader topic of reconstructive memory.  In addition, the introduction justifies the relevance by explaining why a teenage sample and a simplified two-verb design can still shed light on the misinformation effect.  This goes beyond mere identification, but the rationale is brief and somewhat generic, so a solid 5 (rather than 6) is appropriate.

Multiple strengths (controlled environment, equal treatment, age homogeneity) and limitations (population validity, sample size, ecological validity, seating arrangement) are identified and each is explained in relation to potential impact on results.  Coverage is systematic though not exhaustive, justifying a 5 in the top band.

Materials are listed and several controlled variables are identified with brief explanations (age to control driving experience, lighting to ensure visibility, identical questionnaires, etc.).  The rationale for material choice is only partly developed, so a conservative 3 within the 3-4 band is awarded.

One concrete modification (use of virtual reality to increase ecological validity) is proposed and it is tied to the stated limitation of low ecological validity.  However, other stated weaknesses (sample size, seating) are not followed by corresponding, justified adjustments.  This meets the âdescribed but not fully linkedâ standardâ3 within the 3-4 band.

The student not only describes the independent-measures design but also explains why it was chosen (to avoid demand characteristics) and notes why repeated measures would be unsuitable.  This explanatory element meets the upper band of 3-4; the detail is adequate but not exceptionally analytical, so 4 is assigned.

Appropriate descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U) statistics are applied correctly for an independent-measures, non-parametric design.  Calculations, critical values and interpretation are accurate.  Minor issues (calling Cohenâs d âtrivialâ despite large value) limit perfection; thus 5 is justified.

annotations.comment: The background description summarizes Loftus and Palmer but lacks a clear explanation of how schema theory directly informs the predicted verb intensity effect. Consider explicitly linking Bartlettâs schema concepts to the verb manipulation.

The schema theory paragraph mentions confirmation bias and stereotypes but does not define these concepts or relate them to the experimental context. Clarify how these constructs impact memory reconstruction in eyewitness testimony.
The inclusion of distractor questions is good, but clarify how they prevent participants from suspecting the study aim without inadvertently affecting recall of the critical speed question.

The aim statement is clear but its relevance to realâworld eyewitness reliability and teenage participants is only implied. Expand on why studying teenagers and limited verb conditions adds value to reconstructive memory research.
Clear identification and operationalization of both the independent variable (verb used) and dependent variable (speed estimate in km/h) demonstrate strong hypothesis formulation.
The research hypothesis states a difference but lacks directional specificity. Explicitly predict that the âsmashedâ condition will yield higher speed estimates to increase test clarity.

Materials are listed comprehensively but lack explanation of how each item controls extraneous variables. Explain, for example, how the interactive whiteboard setup ensures consistency for all participants.
The convenience sampling rationale is clear but you should acknowledge potential sampling bias from using a single class and discuss its impact on generalizability.
The choice of an independent-measures design is well justified to reduce demand characteristics, demonstrating understanding of design suitability.
Use of random paper assignment for group allocation is a robust method to prevent bias and supports internal validity through randomization.
Ethical considerations are discussed, but strengthen this section by referencing formal ethics approval or oversight procedures.
The list of controlled extraneous variables is comprehensive and demonstrates careful planning to minimize alternative explanations.
Gender is listed as controlled but without justification of its influence on speed estimates. Explain why male/female differences are unlikely to affect reconstructive memory in this task.
Lighting control is noted, but specifying measured brightness (e.g., lux) would improve precision and repeatability of the environmental control.
Statistical methods are well reported, but consider assessing distribution normality or outliers to fully justify the choice of mean over median or mode.
The bar chart clearly displays mean speeds with labels and units, directly addressing the hypothesis and enhancing visual clarity.
Interpretation links to the hypothesis but omits effect size discussion. Include Cohenâs d or another measure to contextualize practical significance.
The descriptive statistics table is well organized, facilitating quick comparison between groups and supporting data transparency.
The Mann-Whitney U result is accurately reported, but explicitly state that the âsmashedâ group ranks higher than âcontactedâ to clarify effect direction.
Briefly discuss the assumptions of the Mann-Whitney U test (e.g., independent samples, similarly shaped distributions) to strengthen the justification for your inferential analysis.
Findings are well tied to reconstructive memory theory, showing how differing verbs reconstruct participantsâ memories in accordance with schema activation.
The discussion introduces confirmation bias and stereotypes without supporting evidence or references. Provide citations or remove unsupported claims to maintain theoretical rigor.
The suggestion to use virtual reality is promising but should explicitly link this modification to the identified ecological validity limitation for full justification.
The independent design strength is well articulated, clearly showing how using two conditions reduces the risk of participants guessing the study aim.
Multiple limitations are identified, but propose specific modificationsâsuch as increasing sample size or adjusting seatingâto directly address these weaknesses and improve reliability.
